summary: This notebook demonstrates a training-free approach to a text classification task by leveraging semantic search. It constructs a corpus from labeled training comments and unlabeled test examples, embeds them using a lightweight Qwen3 embedding model, and predicts labels by aggregating similarity scores of nearest neighbors within the same rule category. The author shows that simple retrieval-based scoring, combined with rule-specific filtering and optional text cleaning, can yield highly competitive leaderboard results in under an hour. Readers should take away that pre-trained embeddings and semantic search can serve as a powerful, fast baseline or feature generator without requiring fine-tuning or complex model architectures.
competition_flow: Loads train and test CSVs, constructs a unified corpus with mapped labels, embeds all text using a pre-trained Qwen3 embedding model, retrieves top-K nearest neighbors per rule, computes a weighted similarity score, and outputs a submission CSV.
data_reading: Reads train.csv and test.csv from a Kaggle input directory using pandas, then flattens train comments and test positive/negative example columns into a single DataFrame.
data_processing: Constructs prompts in the format r/{subreddit}\nComment: {body}, applies text cleaning via the cleantext library (removing URLs, emails, phone numbers, normalizing unicode/ascii), maps labels to 1/-1 for scoring, and normalizes embeddings during inference.
insights: Pre-trained embedding models combined with semantic search can achieve strong competitive performance without any fine-tuning.
Filtering neighbors within the same rule category significantly reduces retrieval noise.
Squaring similarity scores emphasizes high-confidence matches and improves ranking quality.
Text normalization and cleaning enhance embedding consistency across noisy social media comments.
